title: Introduction to Functions
blurb: What makes a relation a function, the f(x) notation, domain and range, and reading real-world graphs — the input–output machines the rest of algebra studies.
buildsOn: Evaluating expressions (Unit 1) and the coordinate plane.

lessons.title: Relations & Functions
lessons.concreteComparison: A vending machine is a function: press B4, get one specific snack. A machine that sometimes drops chips and sometimes a soda for the SAME button is broken — and a relation that sends one input to two outputs is not a function.
lessons.objective: Decide whether a relation (as a set of pairs, a table, a mapping, or a graph) is a function, using the one-output rule and the vertical line test.
concept: A **relation** is any set of input–output pairs. A **function** is a relation with a promise: **every input has exactly ONE output.** Inputs may share an output; an input may never split between two.
Test a set of pairs by scanning the inputs: $\{(1, 4), (2, 4), (3, 5)\}$ is a function (repeated OUTPUT 4 is fine); $\{(1, 4), (1, 5), (2, 6)\}$ is not — input 1 goes two places.

On a graph, the promise becomes geometry: the **vertical line test**. If some vertical line crosses the graph twice, one $x$ has two $y$'s — not a function. Every non-vertical line passes; a circle fails.
lessons.keyIdea: Function = every input gets exactly one output; on a graph, no vertical line may hit the curve twice.

facts.title: The one-output rule
facts.explanation: Repeated outputs are legal; a split input is not.

facts.title: Vertical line test
facts.explanation: A vertical line marks one input; two hits = two outputs for it.

workedExamples.solution: Scan inputs: 2 appears twice, paired with 3 AND with 9 — one input, two outputs. Not a function. (The repeated output 3 at inputs 2 and 8 would have been fine.)

workedExamples.statement: A table pairs each student ID with that student's height. Is height a function of ID? Is ID a function of height?
workedExamples.solution: Each ID belongs to one student with one height — a function. The reverse can fail: two students (two IDs) can share the height 165 cm, so one input (165) would have two outputs. Not a function in reverse.

commonMistakes.correction: Outputs may repeat freely — a constant function sends EVERY input to the same output. Only split inputs break the rule.

commonMistakes.text: Applying a horizontal line test to check for a function.
commonMistakes.correction: The function test is VERTICAL lines (one input, one output). Horizontal lines test a different property (one-to-one).

tryIt.solution: $A$: inputs 0, 1, 2 all distinct — function. $B$: input 3 splits to 0 and 1 — not a function.

tryIt.statement: The graph of $x = y^2$ contains $(4, 2)$ and $(4, -2)$. Is it the graph of a function of $x$?
tryIt.solution: The vertical line $x = 4$ hits both points — input 4 has outputs 2 and $-2$. Not a function of $x$.

steps.title: Machines you can trust
steps.body: Press B4 on a vending machine and you expect ONE specific snack — every time. A button that randomly gives chips or soda is broken. Algebra's most important objects are exactly the trustworthy machines: **relations where each input produces one guaranteed output.** We call them functions.

steps.title: One input, one output
steps.body: A function may reuse outputs — buttons B4 and C1 can both drop chips. What it may NEVER do is split an input: B4 dropping chips today and soda tomorrow. Check pairs by scanning the INPUTS only: any repeat input with different outputs kills it.

steps.title: Spot the broken machine
steps.prompt: Which set is NOT a function?
steps.explanation: Input 1 goes to both 2 and 3 — a split input. The all-outputs-equal set is a perfectly good (constant) function.

steps.title: The vertical line test
steps.body: A vertical line collects all points with ONE particular $x$. If it crosses the graph twice, that input has two outputs — promise broken. Slide an imaginary vertical line across the whole graph: parabolas opening up pass; circles and sideways parabolas fail.

steps.title: Two points, one column
steps.teach: These two points share $x = 4$: they sit in the same vertical column. Any graph through BOTH cannot be a function — the input 4 would have two outputs, 2 and $-2$.

steps.title: Pass or fail?
steps.prompt: Which graph FAILS the vertical line test?
steps.explanation: A vertical line through a circle's middle hits it twice — two outputs for one input. The others are hit at most once by any vertical line.

steps.title: Say it as a sentence
steps.body: For real-world relations, phrase it: 'is ___ a function of ___?' Height is a function of student ID (each ID → one height); ID is NOT a function of height (two students can share 165 cm). The direction matters — always name which is the input.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Function: every input → exactly one output; repeated outputs are fine.
Test pairs by scanning inputs; test graphs with vertical lines.
Direction matters: 'y is a function of x' names x as the input.

lessons.title: Function Notation
lessons.concreteComparison: $f(x)$ is a machine with a name badge. $f(3) = 11$ reads 'feed the $f$-machine a 3 and it returns 11.' The parentheses are a feeding slot, not multiplication.
lessons.objective: Evaluate functions written in f(x) notation (at numbers and at expressions) and solve f(x) = k for the input.
concept: $f(x) = 2x + 5$ names a machine $f$ whose recipe is 'double, add 5.' **$f(3)$ means: run the machine on 3** — replace every $x$ with 3: $f(3) = 11$. The notation is NOT '$f$ times $x$'.

Two directions of question: **evaluate** $f(3)$ (input known → find output) and **solve** $f(x) = 25$ (output known → find input: $2x + 5 = 25$, so $x = 10$). Reading which direction a problem asks is half the skill.
Inputs can be expressions: $f(a + 1) = 2(a + 1) + 5 = 2a + 7$. Substitute the WHOLE input, in parentheses, everywhere $x$ appears.
lessons.keyIdea: f(3) means 'run machine f on input 3'; f(x) = k asks which input produces output k — evaluating and solving are the two directions of one notation.

facts.title: Evaluate = substitute
facts.explanation: Replace every $x$ by the input, in parentheses.

facts.title: Solve = find the input
facts.explanation: The output is given; the equation recovers the input.

commonMistakes.correction: $f$ is a machine, not a number — $f(3)$ is the machine's OUTPUT at input 3. The parentheses are a feeding slot.

commonMistakes.text: Answering $f(x) = 29$ with the output ('29') instead of the input.
commonMistakes.correction: The 29 is given; the question asks WHICH $x$ produced it. Solve the equation: $x = 9$.

steps.title: Machines need names
steps.body: With several recipes in play — one for cost, one for profit, one for tax — 'the expression' stops being a usable name. Function notation gives each machine a badge: $C(x)$ for cost, $P(x)$ for profit. $C(40)$ instantly means 'the cost when $x = 40$.' Compact, precise, universal.

steps.title: The feeding slot
steps.body: $f(x) = 2x + 5$, then $f(3)$: put 3 into the slot — replace every $x$: $f(3) = 2(3) + 5 = 11$. **The parentheses mean 'input goes here', never multiplication.** Output of $f$ at 3, written $f(3)$, equals 11.

steps.title: Feed the machine
steps.teach: This is $f(x) = 2x + 5$ as a live machine: slide the input and watch the output. $f(0) = 5$, $f(4) = 13$ — the notation just names these input–output snapshots.

steps.title: Evaluate
steps.explanation: $(-3)^2 + 1 = 9 + 1 = 10$. Getting $-8$ means the substitution lost its parentheses ($-3^2 + 1$).

steps.title: Given the output, find the input
steps.body: $f(x) = 29$ flips the question: the machine RETURNED 29 — what went in? Set the recipe equal to 29 and solve: $4x - 7 = 29$, so $x = 9$. Evaluate = run forward; solve = run backward. Exams love asking one and receiving the other — read carefully.

steps.title: Which question is it?
steps.prompt: For $g(x) = 5x - 3$: 'find $x$ so that $g(x) = 17$.' The answer is:
steps.explanation: Output 17 is given: $5x - 3 = 17 \to x = 4$. Computing $g(17)$ answers the OTHER direction.

steps.title: Whole input, in parentheses
steps.body: For $f(a + 4)$, the entire package $a + 4$ enters the slot: $3(a + 4) + 2$, parentheses intact, THEN simplify. Dropping the parentheses ($3a + 4 + 2$) feeds the machine only part of its meal.

steps.title: Euler's notation
steps.body: The $f(x)$ notation was introduced by Leonhard Euler in 1734. It's arguably the most successful piece of notation in mathematics — the same symbols appear today in every language, every textbook, and every programming language's functions.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $f(3)$ = output of machine $f$ at input 3 — never multiplication.
Evaluate runs forward; $f(x) = k$ runs backward (solve for $x$).
Expression inputs enter whole, wrapped in parentheses.

lessons.title: Domain & Range
lessons.concreteComparison: A ticket machine takes coins from 10¢ to \$2 — that's its domain, what it ACCEPTS. It prints tickets worth \$1 to \$20 — that's its range, what it PRODUCES. Every function has an accepts-list and a produces-list.
lessons.objective: Find the domain and range of a function from a set of pairs, a graph, or a real-world context, and spot inputs a formula must exclude.
concept: The **domain** is the set of allowed INPUTS; the **range** is the set of outputs actually PRODUCED. For pairs $\{(1, 4), (2, 4), (3, 5)\}$: domain $\{1, 2, 3\}$, range $\{4, 5\}$ — the repeated 4 is listed once.
From a graph, the domain is the graph's SHADOW on the $x$-axis and the range its shadow on the $y$-axis. For $y = x^2$: domain all real numbers; range $y \ge 0$ (a square is never negative).

Formulas exclude two things in Algebra 1: division by zero — $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x - 3}$ bans $x = 3$ — and square roots of negatives — $g(x) = \sqrt{x - 2}$ demands $x \ge 2$. Context restricts further: 'tickets sold' can't be $-5$ or $2.7$.
lessons.keyIdea: Domain = allowed inputs (watch zero denominators, negative radicands, and context); range = outputs actually produced — the graph's shadows on the two axes.

facts.title: Two shadows
facts.explanation: Project the graph down and sideways.

facts.title: The two banned inputs
facts.explanation: No dividing by zero; no square roots of negatives (in Algebra 1).

workedExamples.solution: The denominator dies at $2x - 8 = 0$, i.e. $x = 4$. Domain: all real numbers except 4.

workedExamples.statement: A drone flies for at most 25 minutes on a full battery, and its height is modeled by $h(t)$ for $0 \le t \le 25$, with heights from 0 up to a peak of 120 m. State the domain and range with units.
workedExamples.solution: Domain: $0 \le t \le 25$ minutes (the inputs that make sense). Range: $0 \le h \le 120$ meters (the heights actually reached).

commonMistakes.text: Swapping the two: reporting outputs as the 'domain.'
commonMistakes.correction: D comes before R alphabetically, x comes before y: Domain ↔ $x$ (inputs), Range ↔ $y$ (outputs).

commonMistakes.text: Giving the range of $y = x^2$ as 'all real numbers.'
commonMistakes.correction: Squares are never negative — the outputs are only $y \ge 0$. The range is what the machine actually PRODUCES, not what $y$ could name.

tryIt.statement: State the domain and range of $\{(-1, 2), (0, 5), (3, 2), (4, 8)\}$.

tryIt.solution: Need $3x - 12 \ge 0$, so $x \ge 4$. Check: $g(4) = 0$ works; $x = 3$ would need $\sqrt{-3}$.

steps.title: What goes in, what comes out
steps.body: Every machine has limits. A ticket machine accepts certain coins and prints certain tickets; a drone flies between 0 and 25 minutes and reaches heights between 0 and 120 m. The **domain** is the accepts-list, the **range** the produces-list — and describing a function honestly means stating both.

steps.title: Collect, then project
steps.body: From pairs: domain = all first coordinates, range = all second coordinates (repeats listed once). From a graph: squash the curve DOWN onto the $x$-axis — that shadow is the domain; squash it SIDEWAYS onto the $y$-axis — that's the range.

steps.title: Shadow on the y-axis
steps.prompt: The graph of $y = x^2$ has range:
steps.explanation: Squares are $\ge 0$, and 0 itself is produced (at $x = 0$) — so $y \ge 0$, inclusive.

steps.title: The two forbidden inputs
steps.body: A formula's domain is 'everything that doesn't break it.' Two things break: **dividing by zero** ($\frac{5}{2x-8}$ bans $x = 4$) and **square-rooting a negative** ($\sqrt{3x-12}$ demands $x \ge 4$). Find the crime scene by setting the denominator $= 0$ or the radicand $\ge 0$.

steps.title: Who's excluded?
steps.explanation: The DENOMINATOR must not vanish: $x + 6 = 0$ at $x = -6$. ($x = -1$ just makes the output 0 — perfectly legal.)

steps.title: Units make it real
steps.body: For applied functions, state domain and range WITH units and a reason: 'domain $0 \le t \le 25$ minutes — the battery's life; range $0 \le h \le 120$ m — the heights reached.' If you can't say why the fence is there, you haven't found the real domain yet.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Formulas ban zero denominators and negative radicands.
Context restricts further — counts are whole, times are non-negative.

lessons.title: Interpreting Graphs
lessons.concreteComparison: A graph of a bike ride tells a story without words: climbing means speeding up the hill... no — rising means the QUANTITY grows. Reading graphs is learning to narrate: where it rises, falls, flattens, and what the intercepts say in real units.
lessons.objective: Read function graphs qualitatively: intercepts, increasing/decreasing intervals, maxima/minima, and translate features into real-world sentences.
concept: Graph features carry meaning: the **$y$-intercept** $f(0)$ is the starting value; an **$x$-intercept** is where the quantity hits zero; **rising** sections mean increasing; **falling** mean decreasing; **flat** means constant; the **highest/lowest points** are maxima/minima.

Units turn features into sentences. If $h(t)$ is a drone's height in meters after $t$ minutes: $h(0) = 30$ → 'launched from 30 m'; $h$ increasing on $0 < t < 4$ → 'climbing for the first 4 minutes'; $h(25) = 0$ → 'landed at minute 25.'
Careful: a graph shows VALUE, not motion. A rising height-graph doesn't say the drone moves right — it says height grows over time. Read the axes first, every time: what is measured, in what units.
lessons.keyIdea: Intercepts, rising/falling stretches, and peaks are the vocabulary; the axes' units turn them into true sentences about the situation.

facts.title: Feature dictionary
facts.explanation: Peaks are maxima, valleys are minima — all read left to right.

facts.title: Axes first
facts.latex: \text{read: what vs. what, in which units}
facts.explanation: Every interpretation error starts with unread axis labels.

workedExamples.statement: A water tank's volume $V(t)$ (liters, $t$ in minutes) starts at $V(0) = 200$, falls to 80 at $t = 6$, stays flat until $t = 10$, then falls to 0 at $t = 14$. Narrate the story.
workedExamples.solution: Start with 200 L. Drain for 6 minutes down to 80 L (losing $\frac{200-80}{6} = 20$ L/min on average). Pause 4 minutes (flat = no change). Drain again, reaching empty at minute 14. The $t$-intercept 14 is 'the tank empties.'

workedExamples.statement: For $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3$: find $f(0)$, the $x$-intercepts, and the minimum point.
workedExamples.solution: $f(0) = 3$ ($y$-intercept). Zeros: $x^2 - 4x + 3 = (x-1)(x-3) = 0$ at $x = 1, 3$. Minimum midway at $x = 2$: $f(2) = 4 - 8 + 3 = -1$, so the low point is $(2, -1)$.

commonMistakes.text: Reading a rising distance–time graph as 'going uphill.'
commonMistakes.correction: The graph plots a VALUE against time, not a landscape. Rising = the value grows — nothing about hills.

commonMistakes.text: Confusing where the maximum IS with what it EQUALS.
commonMistakes.correction: 'The max occurs at $x = 2$' names the input; 'the max is $-1$' names the output. Say both: maximum value $-1$ at $x = 2$.

tryIt.statement: For $g(x) = -x^2 + 6x - 5$, find the zeros and the maximum point.
tryIt.solution: Zeros: $-(x^2 - 6x + 5) = -(x-1)(x-5)$, so $x = 1, 5$. Peak midway at $x = 3$: $g(3) = -9 + 18 - 5 = 4$. Maximum value 4 at $x = 3$.

tryIt.statement: A phone battery $B(t)$ (%) falls from $B(0) = 80$ to $B(3) = 20$ over 3 hours of gaming, then charges back to 100 by $t = 5$. When was the battery lowest, and what was the average drain rate while gaming?
tryIt.solution: Lowest at $t = 3$ (20%). Average drain: $\dfrac{80 - 20}{3} = 20$ percentage points per hour.

steps.title: Graphs are stories
steps.body: One picture of $V(t)$ — a tank's volume over time — tells you when it drained, how fast, when someone paused it, and when it ran dry. No sentences needed. Exams test whether you can translate BOTH ways: graph → story and story → graph.

steps.title: Five features
steps.body: **$y$-intercept** $f(0)$: the starting value. **$x$-intercepts**: where the value is zero. **Rising / falling / flat**: increasing, decreasing, constant. **Peaks and valleys**: maxima and minima. Every graph question is some combination of these five, dressed in units.

steps.title: Flat means…
steps.prompt: On a distance–time graph of a cyclist, a FLAT (horizontal) stretch means:
options: the cyclist moves at constant speed
steps.explanation: Flat = distance not changing = not moving. (Constant SPEED would be a rising straight line.) The graph plots distance, not terrain.

steps.title: A parabola's features
steps.teach: These points lie on $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3$. Find the story in the dots: it crosses zero at $x = 1$ and $x = 3$, starts at $(0, 3)$, and bottoms out at $(2, -1)$ — falling before $x = 2$, rising after.

steps.title: Where vs. what
steps.prompt: A profit graph peaks at the point $(40, 2600)$ — price on the x-axis in dollars, profit on the y-axis. The maximum PROFIT is:
steps.explanation: The output (height) is the profit: \$2600. The input 40 is WHERE it happens — the price that achieves it.

steps.title: Axes before answers
steps.body: Before answering anything about a graph, say the axes out loud: 'height in meters versus time in minutes.' Ten seconds of axis-reading prevents the classic disasters — treating value graphs as maps, or mixing up where a maximum occurs with what it equals.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $f(0)$ starts the story; $x$-intercepts are the zeros; rise/fall = inc/dec.
Maximum VALUE is the output; it OCCURS at the input.
Read the axes first — the units write your sentences.

practice.statement: Is $\{(1, 5), (2, 5), (3, 7), (4, 5)\}$ a function? State its domain and range.
practice.solution: Yes — all inputs distinct. Domain $\{1,2,3,4\}$, range $\{5, 7\}$.

practice.statement: For $f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 8$, find the zeros and the minimum point.
practice.solution: $(x-2)(x-4) = 0$: zeros 2 and 4. Vertex midway at $x = 3$: $f(3) = 9 - 18 + 8 = -1$; minimum $(3, -1)$.

testYourself.statement: Which input breaks $\{(0,1), (2,3), (2,5), (6,7)\}$ as a function?
testYourself.solution: Input 2 maps to both 3 and 5 — the split input.
Denominator zero at $x = \pm 5$: all reals except $5$ and $-5$.
$[4(x+2) + 1] - [4x + 1] = 8$ — the outputs rise by 8 for every 2-step in $x$. Check at $x = 3$: $f(5) - f(3) = 21 - 13 = 8$. ✓

testYourself.statement: A bakery's daily profit $P(x)$ (in dollars, $x$ = price per bun) peaks at $(3, 450)$ and has zeros at $x = 1$ and $x = 5$. Interpret all three features.
testYourself.solution: Charging \$3 per bun maximizes profit at \$450/day. At \$1 or \$5 per bun the bakery breaks even ($P = 0$) — too cheap earns nothing per bun, too dear scares buyers away.
